h1Title: Glucocorticoid-Remediable Aldosteronism: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism (GRA), also called familial hyperaldosteronism type I, is a rare autosomal dominant cause of inherited hypertension characterized by aldosterone overproduction that is driven by adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) rather than by the renin-angiotensin system. The underlying mechanism is a chimeric gene formed by fusion of the ACTH-responsive promoter of the 11-beta-hydroxylase gene to the coding sequence of the aldosterone synthase gene — causing aldosterone synthase to be ectopically expressed in the zona fasciculata of the adrenal cortex and activated by ACTH regardless of sodium status or plasma renin activity. Clinically, GRA presents with early-onset hypertension, hypokalemia, and low plasma renin activity; aldosterone secretion is suppressible by exogenous glucocorticoids, which is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

keyStats.prevalence: GRA is rare, estimated at 1 in 100,000 individuals; accounts for approximately 1-2% of all primary aldosteronism cases and less than 1% of all hypertension
keyStats.demographics: Autosomal dominant inheritance — affects males and females equally; presents in childhood or early adulthood (typically before age 20); family history of early-onset hypertension is common
keyStats.avgAge: Presentation typically in childhood or early adulthood (first or second decade); milder cases may remain undiagnosed until middle age when complications of hypertension appear
keyStats.globalCases: Rare globally; estimated few thousand cases described worldwide; may be underdiagnosed due to overlap with essential hypertension and other secondary hypertension causes

definition: Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism (ICD-10: E26.02) is a genetic form of primary aldosteronism caused by a chimeric gene duplication on chromosome 8q24.3. The chimeric gene fuses the ACTH-responsive promoter region of CYP11B1 (11-beta-hydroxylase) to the coding sequence of CYP11B2 (aldosterone synthase), placing aldosterone synthase under ACTH control instead of renin-angiotensin system control. This means ACTH — normally a pituitary hormone regulating cortisol — drives aldosterone production throughout the adrenal cortex, including the zona fasciculata where cortisol is normally produced. The result is: (1) ACTH-driven aldosterone excess causing sodium retention, potassium wasting, and hypertension; (2) suppressed plasma renin activity because sodium retention and volume expansion switch off the renin-angiotensin system; (3) variable cortisol excess because the chimeric enzyme also has some 11-beta-hydroxylase activity. Critically, aldosterone production is suppressible by exogenous glucocorticoids — giving dexamethasone suppresses ACTH, which suppresses the chimeric gene, which reduces aldosterone secretion, confirming the diagnosis and providing the definitive treatment.

typesClassification.description: Complete chimeric duplication of CYP11B1 promoter fused to CYP11B2 coding sequence — causes robust ACTH-driven aldosterone production with early-onset hypertension and hypokalemia. Aldosterone excess is fully suppressible by low-dose dexamethasone.

Partial gene duplication or mosaicism producing milder aldosterone excess — may present with milder hypertension, intermittent hypokalemia, or normokalemia. Diagnosis requires genetic testing. May be more common than classic GRA but underrecognized.

The chimeric enzyme retains some 11-beta-hydroxylase activity, producing mild cortisol excess in addition to aldosterone excess. This can cause additional clinical features such as precocious puberty in children or androgen excess in women.

primarySymptoms: Early-onset hypertension — patients typically develop elevated blood pressure before age 20, often in childhood or adolescence, and may be resistant to standard antihypertensive regimens.
Hypokalemia — potassium wasting from aldosterone excess causes serum potassium below 3.5 mEq/L; may be intermittent or persistent, and can be severe (below 3.0 mEq/L) in some patients.

Fatigue and muscle weakness — hypokalemia from potassium wasting causes generalized muscle weakness, cramping, and fatigue; some patients may have polyuria and polydipsia from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus triggered by hypokalemia.
Headache — chronic hypertension commonly causes headaches, particularly in the morning, and may be the presenting symptom that leads to medical evaluation.

Asymptomatic presentation — many patients, particularly those with milder disease, have no symptoms and are identified incidentally through blood pressure screening or family screening after a relative is diagnosed.

Palpitations — aldosterone-driven hypokalemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias including atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and premature ventricular contractions; palpitations may be the presenting symptom in some patients.
Growth retardation in children — the combination of hypertension and cortisol excess (from partial 11-beta-hydroxylase activity) can impair linear growth in affected children.
earlyWarningSigns: Blood pressure above 130/80 mmHg in a child or young adult — any hypertension before age 20 warrants investigation for secondary causes including GRA

Family history of early-onset hypertension or stroke in family members under 40 — GRA is autosomal dominant; first-degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50% chance of carrying the mutation
Hypokalemia with hypertension — the combination of low potassium and elevated blood pressure should always raise the possibility of primary aldosteronism, including GRA
Hypertension unresponsive to standard antihypertensive medications — GRA patients often require multiple medications; resistance to treatment is a clue
Mild hypernatremia (serum sodium at upper limit of normal) with hypertension — aldosterone causes sodium retention, keeping serum sodium at the high-normal or mildly elevated range
emergencySigns: Severe hypertension (BP above 180/120 mmHg) with neurological symptoms — hypertensive emergency can occur in GRA patients; presents with severe headache, visual changes, confusion, focal deficits, or seizures
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response — hypokalemia potentiates arrhythmias; patients with GRA and hypokalemia are at risk for atrial fibrillation with hemodynamic compromise
Muscle paralysis or severe weakness — severe hypokalemia (potassium below 2.5 mEq/L) can cause flaccid paralysis mimicking periodic paralysis syndromes

Hypertensive encephalopathy — severely elevated blood pressure causing altered mental status, seizures, or focal neurological deficits — requires urgent blood pressure reduction and end-organ protection
Stroke (ischemic or hemorrhagic) — chronic uncontrolled hypertension in GRA increases stroke risk, particularly in younger patients; sudden focal deficits require urgent evaluation

causes.description: GRA is caused by an unequal crossover event during meiosis that duplicates a segment of chromosome 8 containing the 11-beta-hydroxylase (CYP11B1) promoter region and fuses it to the aldosterone synthase (CYP11B2) coding sequence. This creates a chimeric gene in which ACTH drives aldosterone production via the ACTH-responsive CYP11B1 promoter. The mutation is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. The chimeric gene encodes a protein with aldosterone synthase activity (converting corticosterone to 18-hydroxycorticosterone to aldosterone) but is expressed in the zona fasciculata under ACTH control rather than in the zona glomerulosa under angiotensin II control.

ACTH normally regulates cortisol synthesis in the zona fasciculata. In GRA, the ectopic expression of aldosterone synthase in this zone means ACTH drives both cortisol and aldosterone production. ACTH levels are normal in GRA, but the hormonal response is pathologically elevated because the regulatory feedback is disrupted — aldosterone secretion continues regardless of volume status or renin-angiotensin activity. This explains why plasma renin is suppressed: the aldosterone excess causes sodium retention and volume expansion, which physiologically turns off renin.

The chimeric gene is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern — each offspring of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation. Penetrance is high — most carriers develop hypertension in childhood or adolescence. There is no parent-of-origin effect and no imprinting. Variable expressivity within families is common, likely due to modifier genes and environmental factors (sodium intake).

In established GRA, conditions that stimulate ACTH — such as physiological stress, illness, surgery, or glucocorticoid deficiency — can transiently worsen aldosterone excess and hypertension. This is why patients should carry medical alert information and why glucocorticoid replacement during stress is essential.

Sodium intake modulates the severity of aldosterone-mediated hypertension — high sodium intake exacerbates volume expansion and hypertension in GRA patients. The pathophysiology explains why GRA patients benefit from salt restriction: aldosterone acts on the distal nephron to retain sodium; limiting sodium intake reduces the volume-dependent component of hypertension.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of GRA or early-onset hypertension
riskFactors.description: Autosomal dominant inheritance means first-degree relatives of a GRA patient have 50% probability of carrying the mutation. Family screening with genetic testing is recommended for all first-degree relatives.

GRA affects both sexes equally, but males may present with more severe hypertension and earlier cardiovascular complications. Sex differences may reflect higher sodium intake and greater body mass in males.

Sodium intake directly exacerbates the volume-dependent hypertension in GRA. High sodium diets accelerate hypertension severity and increase cardiovascular risk. Sodium restriction is a cornerstone of GRA management.

Conditions that increase endogenous ACTH (stress, infection, surgery, trauma) transiently worsen aldosterone secretion in GRA patients. Patients must be educated about stress dose glucocorticoid coverage during illness to prevent acute exacerbation.
Obesity and metabolic syndrome interact with GRA pathophysiology to worsen hypertension and cardiovascular risk. Weight reduction improves blood pressure control in affected patients.
Hypertension developing before age 20 is itself a risk factor for secondary causes — any young patient with elevated blood pressure should be screened for GRA and other secondary causes.

affectedDemographics: GRA affects males and females equally with autosomal dominant inheritance; estimated prevalence 1 in 100,000 — likely underdiagnosed
Presentation is typically in childhood or early adulthood; mild cases may present in middle age with resistant hypertension
Geographic distribution is global with no ethnic predilection — the chimeric gene arises de novo in diverse populations
Family clustering is prominent — multiple affected individuals within a family are the norm, not the exception, due to autosomal dominant inheritance
diagnosisOverview: GRA is diagnosed through a combination of biochemical testing and genetic confirmation. The diagnostic pathway begins with finding hypokalemia with hypertension and low plasma renin activity (suggesting primary aldosteronism), then proceeds to confirm aldosterone excess (elevated aldosterone), and finally demonstrates glucocorticoid suppressibility (aldosterone falling with dexamethasone administration) and genetic confirmation. The confirmatory test is the dexamethasone suppression test: 0.5 mg dexamethasone every 6 hours for 4 days with aldosterone measured on day 4 — aldosterone falling below 4 ng/dL confirms GRA (aldosterone is normally ACTH-driven, so suppressing ACTH eliminates the stimulus). Genetic testing for the CYP11B1/CYP11B2 chimeric gene by PCR or Southern blot is definitive and has replaced the dexamethasone suppression test as the gold standard diagnostic method in many centers. All first-degree relatives of a confirmed GRA patient should be offered genetic testing, regardless of whether they have hypertension — early detection allows treatment before end-organ damage occurs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Hypokalemia is the classic clue to aldosterone excess — aldosterone drives potassium secretion in the distal nephron. In GRA, hypokalemia may be intermittent or persistent; up to 50% of patients have normal potassium at any single measurement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Normal: 3.5-5.0 mEq/L. In GRA, potassium is typically 2.8-3.5 mEq/L but may be normal between measurements. The absence of hypokalemia does not exclude GRA.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma renin activity (PRA) and plasma renin concentration (PRC)
diagnosticTests.purpose: PRA measures the enzymatic activity of renin in the plasma (ng/mL/hour); PRC measures renin protein concentration. In GRA, aldosterone-driven volume expansion suppresses renin — PRA is low (<1 ng/mL/hour) or suppressed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw collected in an EDTA tube on ice and processed rapidly. Requires patient to be upright (seated 15-30 minutes) before draw. Results within 1-2 days. Suppressed renin with elevated aldosterone confirms primary aldosteronism.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma aldosterone concentration (PAC) and aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated aldosterone with suppressed renin gives a high aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) — the screening test for primary aldosteronism. ARR above 30 (aldosterone in ng/dL divided by PRC in ng/mL/hour above 30) is suggestive; above 100 is highly suggestive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Simultaneous blood draw for aldosterone and renin; must be done in a seated position after 15-30 minutes of ambulation. Results in 1-2 days. A high ARR suggests primary aldosteronism but does not distinguish GRA from other causes (adenoma, bilateral adrenal hyperplasia).

diagnosticTests.purpose: The classic functional confirmation test for GRA — dexamethasone suppresses ACTH (via glucocorticoid receptor negative feedback), which suppresses the ACTH-driven chimeric aldosterone synthase, causing aldosterone to fall to undetectable levels. A positive test is diagnostic.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 0.5 mg dexamethasone every 6 hours for 4 days; plasma aldosterone measured on day 4 after overnight fast. Aldosterone below 4 ng/dL on day 4 confirms GRA. May cause mild hypokalemia worsening during the test — monitor potassium daily.

diagnosticTests.test: CYP11B1/CYP11B2 chimeric gene PCR or Southern blot
diagnosticTests.purpose: Genetic confirmation of the chimeric gene duplication — the definitive diagnostic test. PCR with specific primers spanning the chimeric junction identifies the fusion; Southern blot provides additional confirmation. Available at specialized endocrinology and genetics centers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw (EDTA) for DNA extraction. PCR results within 1-2 weeks. Southern blot takes 3-4 weeks. Sensitivity and specificity are both above 98%. Should be offered to all patients with confirmed primary aldosteronism and a family history of early-onset hypertension.

diagnosticTests.purpose: CT of the adrenal glands is normal in GRA — the chimeric gene causes bilateral adrenal hyperplasia, not a unilateral adenoma. Imaging helps distinguish GRA from aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn syndrome), which shows a unilateral adrenal nodule.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT abdomen with adrenal protocol (thin cuts through adrenal glands). No contrast needed if concerned about renal function. Non-invasive, takes 10-15 minutes. May show bilateral adrenal enlargement or normal glands in GRA.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for determining whether aldosterone excess is unilateral (adenoma) or bilateral (hyperplasia). In GRA, AVS shows bilateral aldosterone secretion suppressed by dexamethasone. Used to distinguish GRA from surgical candidates with aldosterone-producing adenoma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Invasive procedure requiring adrenal vein catheterization by an interventional radiologist. Simultaneous adrenal vein and iliac vein blood samples for aldosterone measurement. Performed at specialized centers. Reserved for cases where surgery is being considered.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of GRA has two goals: controlling blood pressure and preventing cardiovascular and metabolic complications. Low-dose glucocorticoids are the definitive treatment — dexamethasone (0.25-0.5 mg at night) or prednisone (2.5-5 mg at night) suppresses ACTH, which suppresses the chimeric gene, normalizing aldosterone, potassium, and blood pressure in most patients. However, glucocorticoid overtreatment causes Cushing syndrome — doses must be titrated to the minimum effective dose. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) are an excellent alternative or adjunct — they block aldosterone's effect at the receptor level and are well-tolerated. Spironolactone is particularly effective but causes gynecomastia in men (anti-androgen effect); eplerenone is a selective alternative with fewer anti-androgenic effects. Amiloride is a simpler alternative that blocks the epithelial sodium channel directly. Lifestyle modifications (low sodium diet, weight management, regular exercise) are essential adjuncts. Blood pressure targets for GRA patients are the same as for other forms of hypertension. Long-term follow-up is critical — untreated GRA carries significant risk of stroke, cardiac hypertrophy, and premature cardiovascular disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone 0.25-0.5 mg at bedtime
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose dexamethasone is the definitive treatment for GRA — suppresses ACTH, which turns off the chimeric gene's aldosterone production, normalizing blood pressure, potassium, and renin. The smallest effective dose should be used (higher doses cause iatrogenic Cushing).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls blood pressure and normalizes electrolytes in the majority of GRA patients within 2-4 weeks. Suppresses aldosterone to normal or below-normal levels. Risk of long-term glucocorticoid excess (weight gain, glucose intolerance, osteoporosis) if doses are too high.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisone 2.5-5 mg at bedtime
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative to dexamethasone with shorter half-life and less potent glucocorticoid effect. Less likely to cause Cushing features at equipotent doses. Slightly less effective at suppressing ACTH than dexamethasone but better tolerated in some patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective at controlling hypertension and hypokalemia in most GRA patients. Requires twice-daily dosing in some patients for full ACTH suppression. Hydrocortisone is generally too weak for adequate suppression in GRA.

medicalTreatments.name: Spironolactone 25-100 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist — blocks the effect of aldosterone at the epithelial sodium channel in the distal nephron. Reduces sodium retention, potassium wasting, and blood pressure. An excellent primary or adjunctive treatment for GRA.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes blood pressure and potassium in 70-80% of primary aldosteronism patients as monotherapy. Particularly effective in GRA because it blocks the downstream effect of aldosterone regardless of what is driving its production. Side effects include gynecomastia, menstrual irregularities, and hyperkalemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Eplerenone 25-100 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Selective mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist with lower affinity for androgen and progesterone receptors compared to spironolactone — causes less gynecomastia in men and fewer menstrual disturbances in women. Equally effective for blood pressure and potassium control in GRA.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar blood pressure and potassium control to spironolactone with better side effect profile. Particularly preferred in younger women of reproductive age where anti-androgenic effects of spironolactone are undesirable.

medicalTreatments.name: Amiloride 5-15 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Direct epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) blocker — acts downstream of the aldosterone receptor to block sodium reabsorption and reduce potassium excretion. A simpler alternative for GRA patients who cannot tolerate spironolactone or eplerenone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls hypokalemia and modestly reduces blood pressure in GRA. Less effective than MR antagonists for blood pressure control. Can be combined with other antihypertensives for additional benefit.

surgicalOptions.name: Unilateral adrenalectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Not appropriate for GRA — GRA causes bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (ACTH-driven aldosterone production from both adrenals), not a unilateral adenoma. Unilateral adrenalectomy does not cure the condition and removes normal adrenal tissue unnecessarily. Surgery is reserved for aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn syndrome), which is a distinct diagnosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Not applicable for GRA — the bilateral nature of the disease means surgery does not eliminate aldosterone excess.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low sodium diet (<2 g/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Sodium restriction reduces the volume-dependent component of hypertension in GRA. Aldosterone drives sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron; reducing dietary sodium decreases volume expansion and improves blood pressure control.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest blood pressure reduction (5-10 mmHg systolic) in most patients. Amplifies the effect of medical therapy. Essential lifestyle modification for all GRA patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Weight reduction and regular aerobic exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and reduces blood pressure in GRA patients with obesity. Regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week) independently reduces blood pressure and improves cardiovascular outcomes.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Weight loss of 5-10% reduces systolic blood pressure by 5-20 mmHg in hypertensive patients. Exercise provides additional blood pressure reduction of 4-8 mmHg and reduces cardiovascular risk.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should manage GRA — they are the specialists trained in genetic hypertension syndromes, aldosterone pathophysiology, and glucocorticoid management. A pediatric endocrinologist is appropriate for affected children. A cardiologist should be involved in managing hypertension complications (left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction) and in evaluating cardiovascular risk. Nephrologists may be involved in managing resistant hypertension and electrolyte disturbances. A genetic counselor is essential for family screening discussions.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an endocrinologist with experience in adrenal hypertension and genetic endocrine disorders. Ask about familiarity with the dexamethasone suppression test, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist management, and genetic testing for GRA. Academic medical centers and tertiary referral centers for hypertension or adrenal disease typically have the most experience. Family screening should be coordinated by a clinical geneticist.
preventionStrategies: Family screening — all first-degree relatives of a confirmed GRA patient should undergo genetic testing regardless of blood pressure status; early detection allows treatment before end-organ damage develops
Lifelong monitoring of blood pressure and electrolytes in affected individuals — GRA is a chronic condition that requires treatment for life

Genetic counseling before childbearing for affected adults — 50% transmission risk means each pregnancy has a 1-in-2 chance of passing the mutation; preimplantation genetic diagnosis is available for families wishing to avoid transmission
lifestyleModifications: Sodium intake below 2 grams per day — read food labels, avoid processed foods, restaurant meals, and high-sodium condiments; cooking at home with fresh ingredients allows better sodium control
Regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week of moderate intensity) — walking, cycling, or swimming reduces blood pressure and improves cardiovascular health
Maintain healthy body weight — weight reduction improves hypertension and metabolic outcomes; even 5-10% weight loss produces meaningful blood pressure reduction
Limit alcohol intake — alcohol raises blood pressure and may interact with antihypertensive medications
Learn stress dose glucocorticoid protocols — if taking glucocorticoids for GRA, increase dose during illness, surgery, or trauma to prevent ACTH surge and aldosterone flare

recommended: Fresh fruits and vegetables in abundance — low in sodium and high in potassium, which helps counterbalance the hypokalemia from aldosterone excess
Lean protein sources (poultry, fish, legumes) — lower in sodium than processed meats
Whole grains (brown rice, quinoa, oats) — lower sodium than refined grains; fiber helps metabolic health
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, spinach, beans) — potassium supplementation may be needed if hypokalemia is severe; discuss with your physician
Low-fat dairy (unsalted varieties) — provides calcium without excessive sodium
avoid: Processed and packaged foods — bread, cheese, canned soups, deli meats, frozen meals, and snack foods are high in hidden sodium
Restaurant and fast food — typically very high in sodium; cooking at home is the best strategy for sodium control
Soy sauce, miso, and other high-sodium condiments — 1 tablespoon of soy sauce contains approximately 900 mg sodium
Cured and fermented foods (ham, bacon, pickles, olives) — very high in sodium
Sports drinks with sodium — unnecessary sodium intake worsens volume expansion; plain water is sufficient for hydration

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week of moderate intensity: brisk walking, cycling, swimming) is strongly recommended for GRA patients. Exercise reduces blood pressure (4-8 mmHg independently), improves insulin sensitivity, reduces cardiovascular risk, and supports weight management. No restrictions on exercise type when blood pressure is controlled — patients with uncontrolled hypertension should avoid heavy resistance training and high-intensity competitive sports until BP is controlled. Light to moderate aerobic exercise is safe even in patients with mild hypokalemia; strenuous exercise should be avoided during periods of significant hypokalemia (potassium below 3.0 mEq/L) due to arrhythmia risk.
dailyManagement: Take glucocorticoid medication (dexamethasone or prednisone) exactly as prescribed — consistency is important; missed doses can cause aldosterone flares and blood pressure spikes
Monitor blood pressure at home — aim for below 130/80 mmHg; record readings to share with your physician at appointments
Check serum potassium periodically — hypokalemia can fluctuate; your physician will advise on testing frequency based on your baseline level
Limit sodium to under 2 grams daily — check food labels, cook at home, avoid restaurant meals
Carry a medical alert card identifying GRA and the need for stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage during illness or surgery
Notify all healthcare providers of GRA diagnosis before any procedure — glucocorticoid coverage prevents ACTH-driven aldosterone surges during stress
prognosis: GRA has an excellent prognosis with appropriate treatment — blood pressure normalizes, hypokalemia resolves, and cardiovascular risk approaches that of the general population when therapy is maintained. Without treatment, GRA causes progressive hypertension with end-organ damage: left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, stroke, and premature cardiovascular events in the second and third decades of life. Early diagnosis through family screening dramatically improves outcomes — children identified through screening and treated from a young age avoid the decades of uncontrolled hypertension that cause irreversible cardiac and vascular damage. Glucocorticoid therapy controls the condition in most patients, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists provide an effective alternative for those who cannot tolerate glucocorticoids. Lifelong treatment is required; there is no cure, only suppression of the phenotype. The autosomal dominant inheritance means each affected patient has a 50% chance of passing the mutation to their children.
recoveryTimeline: Blood pressure begins to fall within 1-2 weeks of starting glucocorticoid or mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist therapy; full normalization typically occurs within 4-8 weeks. Serum potassium rises to normal range within 1-2 weeks of treatment initiation. Left ventricular hypertrophy regression occurs gradually over 6-24 months with sustained blood pressure control. Patients transitioning from untreated to treated state often notice improved energy, reduced headaches, and normalization of muscle strength as hypokalemia resolves.
complications: Stroke — hemorrhagic or ischemic stroke is the most serious complication of untreated GRA; the combination of severe hypertension and aldosterone-induced endothelial dysfunction dramatically elevates stroke risk, particularly in younger patients

Left ventricular hypertrophy and heart failure — chronic hypertension causes cardiac remodeling; aldosterone independently promotes cardiac fibrosis. Treated GRA patients have much lower cardiac morbidity.

Atrial fibrillation and cardiac arrhythmias — hypokalemia potentiates atrial fibrillation; GRA patients with hypokalemia have elevated arrhythmia risk. Control of hypokalemia substantially reduces this risk.
Chronic kidney disease — aldosterone and hypertension cause glomerular hyperfiltration and progressive kidney damage over years; early treatment preserves renal function

Hypokalemic periodic paralysis — severe hypokalemia (potassium below 2.5 mEq/L) can present as episodic flaccid paralysis mimicking genetic periodic paralysis; correcting potassium resolves the episodes

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guidelines on Primary Aldosteronism
supportResources.description: Evidence-based guidelines on the diagnosis and management of primary aldosteronism, including GRA, from the Endocrine Society. Includes recommendation on family screening for familial forms.

supportResources.name: NIH Genetics Home Reference — Glucocorticoid-Remediable Aldosteronism
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly explanation of GRA genetics, inheritance, diagnosis, and management from the National Library of Medicine.

supportResources.name: American Society of Hypertension
supportResources.description: Resources on hypertension management, including information on secondary causes and refractory hypertension.

confusedWithConditions.name: Aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn syndrome)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause hypokalemia, hypertension, and low renin, but aldosterone-producing adenoma is a unilateral benign tumor treated surgically — imaging shows a unilateral adrenal nodule. GRA is bilateral adrenal hyperplasia driven by ACTH, not treatable surgically. The dexamethasone suppression test is positive in GRA (aldosterone falls) but not in adenoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Essential Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Essential hypertension typically presents later (after age 40-50), does not cause hypokalemia, and renin is normal or elevated (not suppressed). Essential hypertension has no family history pattern of early-onset stroke. GRA presents before age 20 with hypokalemia and suppressed renin, plus a family history of early-onset hypertension.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renovascular Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Renovascular hypertension (from renal artery stenosis) causes hypertension with HIGH renin — the kidney perceives reduced perfusion and increases renin secretion. GRA has suppressed renin. The two are on opposite ends of the renin spectrum — this distinction is fundamental.

confusedWithConditions.name: Liddle Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Liddle syndrome is another autosomal dominant cause of low-renin hypertension but is caused by mutations in the epithelial sodium channel (SCNN1B/SCNN1G) rather than aldosterone excess. Liddle syndrome has normal aldosterone levels, suppressed renin, and hypokalemia — but does not respond to spironolactone (because the ENaC mutation bypasses the aldosterone receptor). Genetic testing distinguishes the two.

relatedConditions.name: Aldosterone-producing Adenoma (Conn Syndrome)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both GRA and aldosterone-producing adenoma cause primary aldosteronism (hypertension, hypokalemia, suppressed renin, elevated aldosterone). However, adenoma is unilateral and treated surgically; GRA is bilateral and treated medically. The distinction is critical — removing one adrenal gland does not cure GRA and leaves the patient with persistent bilateral disease and only one remaining adrenal.

relatedConditions.name: Liddle Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Liddle syndrome and GRA are the two main genetic causes of low-renin hypertension with hypokalemia. Both are autosomal dominant and present early. Distinguishing them matters because treatment differs: Liddle syndrome responds to amiloride and ENaC blockers but not to spironolactone; GRA responds to spironolactone, MR antagonists, and glucocorticoids. Genetic testing is definitive.

relatedConditions.name: Hypokalemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypokalemia is a direct consequence of aldosterone excess — aldosterone drives renal potassium wasting. All GRA patients have some degree of hypokalemia, though it may be intermittent. Correction of hypokalemia with treatment is a key measure of therapeutic success.

faqs.question: What is glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism?
faqs.answer: GRA is a genetic form of hypertension where a mutation (chimeric gene fusing ACTH-responsive promoter to aldosterone synthase) causes aldosterone to be produced in response to ACTH instead of the renin-angiotensin system. The key feature is that aldosterone production is suppressible by exogenous glucocorticoids (dexamethasone), which is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

faqs.question: How is GRA treated?
faqs.answer: Low-dose glucocorticoids (dexamethasone 0.25-0.5 mg or prednisone 2.5-5 mg at bedtime) suppress ACTH, turning off the chimeric gene and normalizing aldosterone and blood pressure. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) are an excellent alternative or adjunct — they block aldosterone's effect directly. Low-sodium diet and weight management are essential lifestyle measures.

faqs.question: Is GRA hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes — GRA is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. Each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation. First-degree relatives should be offered genetic testing regardless of blood pressure status. Family screening identifies affected individuals early, before end-organ damage develops.

faqs.question: What is the difference between GRA and Conn syndrome?
faqs.answer: Both cause hypertension, hypokalemia, and low renin, but GRA is a bilateral genetic condition treated medically with glucocorticoids or MR antagonists. Conn syndrome (aldosterone-producing adenoma) is a unilateral tumor treated surgically by adrenalectomy. CT shows a nodule in Conn syndrome; genetic testing is positive in GRA.

faqs.question: What causes the hypertension in GRA?
faqs.answer: Aldosterone excess causes sodium retention and volume expansion, raising blood pressure. The volume expansion suppresses renin (the kidneys perceive adequate volume), so the usual renin feedback loop is switched off. Aldosterone also promotes cardiac fibrosis and endothelial dysfunction independent of blood pressure effects.

faqs.question: Can GRA be cured?
faqs.answer: No — GRA is a genetic condition and cannot be cured. Treatment is lifelong and involves either low-dose glucocorticoids or mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists to suppress aldosterone's effects. With treatment, blood pressure normalizes, hypokalemia resolves, and long-term outcomes are excellent. Untreated GRA causes progressive cardiovascular damage.

faqs.question: What is the aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR)?
faqs.answer: The ARR is the screening test for primary aldosteronism — aldosterone (ng/dL) divided by renin (ng/mL/hour or PRC in ng/mL). A ratio above 30 is suggestive and above 100 is highly suggestive of aldosterone excess. In GRA, the ratio is very high because aldosterone is elevated and renin is suppressed. The ARR does not distinguish between GRA and other causes of primary aldosteronism.

faqs.question: Does GRA cause hypokalemia?
faqs.answer: Yes — aldosterone drives potassium secretion in the distal nephron. GRA patients typically have mild-to-moderate hypokalemia (potassium 2.8-3.5 mEq/L), though it may be intermittent. Some patients maintain normal potassium, particularly those with higher dietary potassium intake. Hypokalemia contributes to muscle weakness, fatigue, and arrhythmia risk.

faqs.question: Why does dexamethasone suppress aldosterone in GRA?
faqs.answer: Dexamethasone suppresses ACTH by activating glucocorticoid receptors in the pituitary (negative feedback). In GRA, the chimeric gene uses the ACTH-responsive promoter from the 11-beta-hydroxylase gene, so when ACTH is suppressed, the gene is turned off and aldosterone production falls. In normal aldosterone-producing adenoma or bilateral hyperplasia, aldosterone is driven by angiotensin II, not ACTH, so dexamethasone has no effect.

faqs.question: Can GRA be diagnosed without genetic testing?
faqs.answer: Yes — the dexamethasone suppression test historically confirmed the diagnosis: aldosterone falling to undetectable levels after 4 days of dexamethasone confirms ACTH-driven aldosterone production (GRA). However, genetic testing for the CYP11B1/CYP11B2 chimeric gene is now the gold standard because it is definitive, non-invasive (simple blood draw), and faster than the suppression test.

faqs.question: What is the risk of stroke in GRA?
faqs.answer: Untreated GRA significantly increases stroke risk — particularly hemorrhagic stroke in younger patients with severe hypertension. The combination of very high blood pressure and aldosterone-induced vascular changes creates a prothrombotic state. Treated patients have dramatically lower stroke risk, approaching that of the general population.

faqs.question: Can GRA patients take regular blood pressure medications?
faqs.answer: GRA patients often need multiple antihypertensives if glucocorticoid or MR antagonist therapy is not fully controlling blood pressure. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, and beta-blockers can all be used as adjuncts. However, spironolactone or eplerenone are more effective than standard antihypertensives because they address the specific pathophysiology.

faqs.question: Does GRA affect fertility?
faqs.answer: GRA does not directly impair fertility. However, women with GRA who become pregnant require careful management — hypertension may worsen during pregnancy, and some antihypertensive medications (spironolactone, ACE inhibitors, ARBs) are contraindicated in pregnancy. Dexamethasone is considered safe in pregnancy. Pre-pregnancy counseling with an endocrinologist and maternal-fetal medicine specialist is recommended.

faqs.question: What happens if a GRA patient has surgery or gets seriously ill?
faqs.answer: Patients with GRA taking glucocorticoids need stress-dose coverage during surgery, trauma, or serious illness — the physiological cortisol surge in stress normally also drives some aldosterone in GRA; without adequate glucocorticoid coverage, ACTH can surge and cause aldosterone spikes and severe hypertension. Patients should carry medical alert information and inform all healthcare providers of their condition.

faqs.question: Is GRA common?
faqs.answer: GRA is rare — estimated 1 in 100,000 individuals. It accounts for only 1-2% of primary aldosteronism cases and a tiny fraction of all hypertension. It is likely underdiagnosed because many cases are mislabeled as essential hypertension or other secondary causes. Family history and early-onset hypertension are the main clues.

faqs.question: What causes the chimeric gene in GRA?
faqs.answer: During meiosis (sperm or egg formation), an unequal crossover event occurs between the CYP11B1 gene (11-beta-hydroxylase, on chromosome 8) and the CYP11B2 gene (aldosterone synthase, also on chromosome 8). This creates a chimeric gene with the regulatory region of CYP11B1 (ACTH-responsive) fused to the coding region of CYP11B2 (aldosterone synthase enzyme). The mutation arises de novo in the affected individual and is then passed autosomal dominantly.

faqs.question: Does GRA cause Cushing syndrome?
faqs.answer: Low-dose dexamethasone treatment for GRA does not cause Cushing syndrome when doses are kept minimal (0.25-0.5 mg dexamethasone or 2.5-5 mg prednisone). However, if doses are excessive, Cushing features (weight gain, moon facies, striae) can develop. The goal is to use the smallest effective glucocorticoid dose — enough to suppress ACTH but not enough to cause Cushing features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between GRA and familial hyperaldosteronism type III?
faqs.answer: Familial hyperaldosteronism type III (FH-III) is a different genetic disorder caused by mutations in the KCNJ5 gene, causing severe early-onset aldosterone excess with massive adrenal hyperplasia. Unlike GRA, aldosterone production in FH-III is NOT suppressible by glucocorticoids — this is the key clinical distinction. FH-III also tends to be more severe and more resistant to treatment.

metaDescription: GRA (familial hyperaldosteronism type I): ACTH-driven aldosterone excess causing early hypertension, hypokalemia, low renin. Dexamethasone suppresses aldosterone. Genetic testing and treatment explained.
